author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
How Could the Incorporeal God Breathe Out of Himself a Corporeal Substance?
title: How Could the Incorporeal God Breathe Out of Himself a Corporeal Substance?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Chapter 9 [V.]—How Could the Incorporeal God Breathe Out of Himself a Corporeal Substance?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: In that he believes God to be truly incorporeal, I congratulate him that herein, at all events, he has kept himself uninfluenced by the ravings of Tertullian.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For he insisted, that as the soul is corporeal, so likewise is God.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: It is therefore specially surprising that our author, who differs from Tertullian in this point, yet labours to persuade us that the incorporeal God does not make the soul out of nothing, but exhales it as a corporeal breath out of Himself.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: What a wonderful learning that must be to which every age erects its attentive ears, and which contrives to gain for its disciples men of advanced years, and even presbyters!

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Let this eminent man read what he has written, read it in public; let him invite to hear the reading well-known persons and unknown ones, learned and unlearned.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Old men, assemble with your younger instructors; learn what you used to know nothing about; hear now what you had never heard before.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Behold, according to the teaching of this scribe, God creates a breath, not out of something else which exists in some way or other, and not out of that which absolutely has no existence; but out of that which He is Himself, perfectly incorporeal, He breathes a body so that He actually changes His own incorporeal nature into a body, before it undergoes the change into the body of sin.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Does he say, that He does not change something out of His own nature, when He creates breath?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Then, of course, He does not make that breath out of Himself: for He is not Himself one thing, and His nature another thing.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: What is this insane man thinking of?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But if he says that God creates breath out of His own nature in such a way as to remain absolutely entire Himself, this is not the question.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: The question is, whether that which comes not of some previously created substance, nor from nothing, but from Him, is not what He is, that is, of the same nature and essence?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Now He remains absolutely entire after the generation of His Son; but because He begat Him of His own nature, He did not beget a something which was different from that which He is Himself.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For, putting to one side the circumstance that the Word took on Himself a human nature and became flesh, the Word who is the Son of God is another but not another thing: that is, He is another person but not a different nature.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: And whence does this come to pass, except from the fact that He is not created out of something else, or out of nothing, but was begotten out of Himself; not that He might be better than He was, but that He might be altogether even what He is of whom He is begotten; that is, of one and the same nature, equal, co-eternal, in every way like, equally unchangeable, equally invisible, equally incorporeal, equally God; in a word, that He might be altogether what the Father is, except that He actually is Himself the Son, and not the Father?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But if He remains Himself the same God entire and unimpaired, but yet creates something different from Himself, and worse than Himself, not out of nothing, nor out of some other creature, but out of His very self; and that something emanates as a body out ofthe incorporeal God; then God forbid that a catholic should imbibe such an opinion, for it does not flow from the divine fountain, but it is a mere fiction of the human mind.
